---
title: "Week 6: LDA Topic Modeling with Nexis Uni Data"
subtitle: "EDS 231 — Spring 2026"
date: "April 28, 2026"
output:
  html_document:
    theme: bootstrap
    highlight: pygments
    css: week-styles.css
    toc: false
    toc_float:
      collapsed: false
      smooth_scroll: true
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

> Applying Latent Dirichlet Allocation to discover latent themes in a news corpus you built from Nexis Uni.

---

### Learning Objectives

By the end of this week, you will be able to:

- Explain the generative assumptions behind Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA)
- Select a value of *k* using theory, optimization metrics (`ldatuning`), and interpretability
- Interpret the beta (word-topic) and theta (topic-document) posterior distributions
- Visualize topic structure with term plots and `{LDAvis}`
- Apply topic modeling to a real news corpus and characterize emergent themes

---

### Recommended Reading

📖 [Text Mining with R — Ch. 6: Topic Modeling](https://www.tidytextmining.com/topicmodeling)

📖 [Blei, Ng & Jordan (2003) — Latent Dirichlet Allocation, *JMLR*](https://jmlr.org/papers/v3/blei03a.html)

---

## Lecture Materials

### Slides

🖥 Slide deck from last week's lecture

[View Slides](#https://ucsb.box.com/s/qge5x1j6gtwh1gd6j0hywc4vv8gjhb8p){.btn .btn-primary}

### Code & Data

💻 R scripts used during class:

[Lab4](https://github.com/MaRo406/EDS-231-text-sentiment/blob/main/Labs/Lab4.Rmd)

---

## 📂 Assignment

### Lab 4 — LDA Topic Modeling <span class="badge badge-danger">Graded · 11 pts</span>

**Due: May 12 @ 11:59 pm**

In this lab you will fit an LDA topic model to the Nexis Uni news corpus you assembled in Week 5. You will tune the number of topics *k*, inspect the resulting word-topic and topic-document distributions, and write a short interpretation of the themes your model discovers.

[Download Lab 4](https://github.com/MaRo406/EDS-231-text-sentiment/blob/main/Labs/Lab4.Rmd){.btn .btn-primary}

---

#### Getting Your Data

Follow the Nexis Uni data retrieval instructions on the [Week 5 page](week-5.html) to download and load your corpus before lab.

> **Note on corpus size:** The Week 5 instructions recommend downloading around 100 articles as a starting point. For topic modeling, however, **more data generally produces cleaner, more stable topics**. If your initial results feel noisy or topics are hard to interpret, consider going back to Nexis Uni and downloading additional articles — bump it up to **500 or so** — to give LDA enough signal to work with.

---

[← Week 5: Topic Modeling Prep](week-5.html)
